"And Aaron the priest went up into mount Hor at the commandment of the LORD, and died there, in the fortieth year after the children of Israel were come out of the land of Egypt, in the first day of the fifth month." The witnesses note the death of Aaron the high priest on mount Hor — the crux of the passing of the first priesthood and the law's priest who could not bring in. On "Aaron the priest went up into mount Hor at the commandment of the LORD, and died there, in the fortieth year," interpreters expound the death. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the high priest's death — "Aaron the priest went up into mount Hor at the commandment of the LORD, and died there, in the fortieth year after the children of Israel were come out of the land of Egypt": at God's command Aaron ascended mount Hor and died there, in the fortieth year of the wandering; the witnesses note the significance — the death of the first high priest, recorded with solemn precision (the very date noted, the fortieth year, the first day of the fifth month, the close of the wilderness pilgrimage near at hand); the lesson — that the Aaronic priesthood was mortal and passing, the high priest himself dying outside the land; the truth that the law's priesthood could not bring the people into the rest, the priests "not suffered to continue by reason of death," Hebrews 7:23; the figure — the contrast with Christ the great High Priest, who "continueth ever, hath an unchangeable priesthood," Hebrews 7:24-25; The figure of the mortal high priest whose death pointed to the eternal Priesthood of Christ.

On "Aaron the priest went up into mount Hor at the commandment of the LORD, and died there," the grammatical-historical and witnesses note the death. On "ye shall drive out all the inhabitants of the land... and destroy all their pictures... molten images... and quite pluck down all their high places," interpreters expound the command. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the purging — "ye shall drive out all the inhabitants of the land from before you, and destroy all their pictures, and destroy all their molten images, and quite pluck down all their high places": Israel was to dispossess the Canaanites and utterly destroy every object and place of their idolatry; the witnesses note the significance — the total purging of the land from idolatry that no snare of idolatry remain); the lesson — that God's people must put away all idolatry without compromise (the command not merely to drive out the people but to destroy the very objects of their false worship, vv.55; the thoroughness of the command, "destroy... destroy... quite pluck down," no idol spared); the land cleansed for his worship; the figure — the call to put away every idol from the heart and life, "little children, keep yourselves from idols," 1 John 5:21; "what agreement hath the temple of God with idols?... come out from among them, and be ye separate," 2 Corinthians 6:16-17; The figure of the total destruction of idols, that God's people be wholly his.

On "ye shall drive out all the inhabitants of the land from before you," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and witnesses note the dispossession. Ellicott and Gill note the Canaanites to be driven out before Israel (the dispossession, the land cleared) — the driving out (the inhabitants).

On "and destroy all their pictures, and destroy all their molten images, and quite pluck down all their high places," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the destruction of idols. Wesley and Gill note the utter destruction of every idol, image, and high place, that no snare of idolatry remain (the purging of the land, the separation from idols, 1 John 5:21; 2 Corinthians 6:16-17) — the figure (the putting away of every idol from the heart); the providence (the charge, the dwelling in the land to follow, vv.53). The verse displays the charge to drive out the inhabitants and destroy all their idolatry.

The witnesses agree the substance: Israel was to drive out the Canaanites and utterly destroy all their idols and high places (Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the purging of the land: the destruction of idols — the inhabitants dispossessed and every object and place of their false worship utterly destroyed, that no snare of idolatry remain, the thoroughness of the command that brooks no compromise with idols, a figure of the believer's call to put away every idol from the heart and to be wholly separate to God (1 John 5:21; 2 Corinthians 6:16-17). The verse displays the purging crux. The charge to drive out the inhabitants and destroy all their idolatry displays the crux: destroy all their pictures... molten images... and quite pluck down all their high places (1 John 5:21; 2 Corinthians 6:16-17) — the total purging of the land from idols, that no snare remain, a figure of the believer's call to put away every idol from the heart and be wholly separate to God. The verse displays the dwelling in the land God gave (v.53).
53:
"And ye shall dispossess the inhabitants of the land, and dwell therein: for I have given you the land to possess it." The very brief verse notes the land given to Israel to possess. The grammatical voices note the gift. Gill notes that "ye shall dispossess the inhabitants of the land, and dwell therein: for I have given you the land to possess it" — Israel was to dispossess the Canaanites and dwell in the land, for God had given it to them; the ye shall dispossess the inhabitants of the land, and dwell therein (the possession); the for I have given you the land to possess it (the gift, the ground of the possession); the figure (the land given by God, to be possessed by his gift; the truth that the inheritance was God's gift, the possession resting on his promise, not on their own strength; the figure of the inheritance given of God, possessed by faith in his gift; the truth that what God has given his people they are to take and hold, the land theirs by his word, Genesis 13:15; the gift that grounds the conquest, the possession of the promise). The verse holds together the land given to possess.

On "ye shall dispossess the inhabitants of the land, and dwell therein," the witness notes the possession. Gill notes Israel dispossessing the foe and dwelling (the possession) — the possession (dwelling in the land).

On "for I have given you the land to possess it," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the gift: "I have given you the land to possess it" — the land God's gift, the ground of the possession (Genesis 13:15); the figure (the inheritance given of God); the providence (the gift, the division by lot to follow, vv.54). The verse displays the land given to Israel to possess.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witness agrees: the gift — "ye shall dispossess the inhabitants of the land, and dwell therein: for I have given you the land to possess it," the gift (Gill); "ye shall dispossess the inhabitants of the land, and dwell therein," the possession (Gill); "for I have given you the land to possess it," the gift (Genesis 13:15, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gift. The verse marks the land: given. The verse displays the inhabitants: dispossessed. The verse displays Israel: dwelling. The verse displays the possession: by God's gift. The gift displays the inheritance given of God. The verse displays the land: God's gift. The verse displays the possession: grounded. The verse displays the promise: fulfilled. The land given to Israel to possess displays the inheritance given of God: I have given you the land to possess it (Genesis 13:15) — the land God's gift, the possession resting on his promise and not on their own strength, the inheritance to be taken and held by his word. The verse displays the division of the land by lot (v.54).
54:
"And ye shall divide the land by lot for an inheritance among your families: and to the more ye shall give the more inheritance, and to the fewer ye shall give the less inheritance: every man's inheritance shall be in the place where his lot falleth; according to the tribes of your fathers ye shall inherit." The witnesses note the division of the land by lot for an inheritance — the crux of the inheritance distributed by God's own appointment. On "ye shall divide the land by lot for an inheritance among your families... every man's inheritance shall be in the place where his lot falleth," interpreters expound the division. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the lot — "ye shall divide the land by lot for an inheritance among your families: and to the more ye shall give the more inheritance, and to the fewer ye shall give the less... every man's inheritance shall be in the place where his lot falleth": the land was to be divided by lot among the tribes and families,; the witnesses note the significance — the inheritance distributed by the lot Proverbs 16:33); the lesson — that God himself assigns to each his portion in the inheritance (the lot the token of God's sovereign distribution); every man's portion fixed by him; the figure — the inheritance of the saints distributed by God, "who hath made us meet to be partakers of the inheritance of the saints in light," Colossians 1:12; "the lines are fallen unto me in pleasant places; yea, I have a goodly heritage," Psalm 16:5-6; The figure of the inheritance distributed by God's own appointment to each his portion.

On "ye shall divide the land by lot for an inheritance among your families," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the lot. Keil and Gill note the land divided by lot among the families (the inheritance by lot, God's appointment, Proverbs 16:33) — the lot (the division).

On "to the more ye shall give the more inheritance, and to the fewer ye shall give the less... every man's inheritance shall be in the place where his lot falleth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the portion. Calvin and Gill note the size by number, the place by the lot, each portion fixed by God (the inheritance of God's appointing, Colossians 1:12; Psalm 16:5-6) — the figure (the inheritance distributed by God to each his portion); the providence (the division, the warning of the remnant to follow, vv.55). The verse displays the division of the land by lot for an inheritance.

The witnesses agree the substance: the land was divided by lot among the tribes and families, the size by number and the place by the lot (Calvin, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the inheritance distributed by God's appointment: the division by lot — the inheritance assigned not by man's will but by the lot, that is, by God's own sovereign disposing (Proverbs 16:33), each tribe and family receiving the place he appointed, a figure of the inheritance of the saints distributed by God (Colossians 1:12) and of the portion he appoints to every one of his people (Psalm 16:5-6). The verse displays the inheritance-by-lot crux. The division of the land by lot for an inheritance displays the crux: every man's inheritance shall be in the place where his lot falleth (Proverbs 16:33; Colossians 1:12; Psalm 16:5-6) — the inheritance distributed by the lot of God's providence, each receiving the portion he appointed, a figure of the inheritance of the saints given by God's sovereign hand. The verse displays the warning of the inhabitants left undriven (v.55).
55:
"But if ye will not drive out the inhabitants of the land from before you; then it shall come to pass, that those which ye let remain of them shall be pricks in your eyes, and thorns in your sides, and shall vex you in the land wherein ye dwell." The witnesses note the warning that the inhabitants left undriven would be pricks and thorns — the crux of the danger of sparing what God commands to destroy. On "those which ye let remain of them shall be pricks in your eyes, and thorns in your sides, and shall vex you," interpreters expound the warning. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the peril of compromise — "if ye will not drive out the inhabitants of the land... then those which ye let remain of them shall be pricks in your eyes, and thorns in your sides, and shall vex you in the land wherein ye dwell": if Israel spared any of the Canaanites, the remnant would become a torment and snare; the witnesses note the significance — the warning against incomplete obedience, the sparing of what God commanded to destroy vv.56); the lesson — the peril of compromise with sin, (the half-obedience that leaves the enemy, the truth that the sin or idol spared will turn and wound, "thorns in your sides," the besetting trouble); the figure — the danger of sparing any sin, Matthew 5:29; the truth, fulfilled in Israel's history, that the Canaanites they spared became their snare and ruin (Judges 2:3, "they shall be as thorns in your sides, and their gods shall be a snare unto you"); The figure of the danger of sparing what God commands to destroy, the remnant become thorns and pricks.

On "if ye will not drive out the inhabitants of the land from before you; then it shall come to pass, that those which ye let remain of them," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and witnesses note the sparing. Ellicott and Gill note the inhabitants left undriven, the half-obedience that spares the foe (the incomplete obedience, Judges 2:3) — the sparing (the remnant left).

On "shall be pricks in your eyes, and thorns in your sides, and shall vex you in the land wherein ye dwell," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the vexation. Wesley and Gill note the spared remnant becoming pricks and thorns, a perpetual vexation and snare (the peril of compromise, the besetting sin, Matthew 5:29; Judges 2:3) — the figure (the danger of sparing any sin, the spared enemy turned tormentor); the providence (the warning, the judgment if they fail to follow, vv.56). The verse displays the warning that the inhabitants left undriven would be pricks and thorns.

The witnesses agree the substance: if Israel spared the inhabitants, the remnant would be pricks and thorns and a perpetual vexation (Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the danger of sparing what God commands to destroy: the thorns and pricks — the inhabitants left undriven becoming a torment and snare, the peril of incomplete obedience that spares the enemy God bids destroy (fulfilled in Israel's history, Judges 2:3), a figure of the besetting sin or idol not wholly mortified that turns to wound and vex the soul (Matthew 5:29). The verse displays the peril-of-compromise crux. The warning that the inhabitants left undriven would be pricks and thorns displays the crux: those which ye let remain of them shall be pricks in your eyes, and thorns in your sides (Judges 2:3; Matthew 5:29) — the peril of incomplete obedience, the spared enemy become a constant torment and snare, a figure of the besetting sin not wholly put away that turns to wound and vex the soul. The verse displays the warning of judgment if they fail (v.56).
56:
"Moreover it shall come to pass, that I shall do unto you, as I thought to do unto them." The very brief verse notes the warning that God would judge Israel as he judged the nations if they sinned like them. The grammatical voices note the threat. Gill notes that "I shall do unto you, as I thought to do unto them" — if Israel imitated the Canaanites in their idolatry, God would do to Israel what he purposed against the nations; the I shall do unto you (the judgment on Israel); the as I thought to do unto them (as God purposed against the Canaanites); the figure (the solemn warning that God is no respecter of persons, and would judge his own people if they sinned like the nations; the truth that the privilege of being God's people is no shield to those who do the abominations of the heathen, "you only have I known of all the families of the earth: therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities," Amos 3:2; the figure of the impartial justice of God, who would cast out his own people for the sins for which he cast out the Canaanites, fulfilled in Israel's later captivity; the warning that closes the chapter, that Israel not presume on their election but obey). The verse closes the chapter, before the borders of the land.

On "I shall do unto you," the witness notes the judgment. Gill notes the judgment threatened on Israel (the warning) — the judgment (on Israel).

On "as I thought to do unto them," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the likeness: "as I thought to do unto them" — as God purposed against the Canaanites (the impartial justice, Amos 3:2); the figure (God judging his own people for the sins of the nations); the providence (the warning, the borders of the land to follow, ch.34). The verse displays the warning that God would judge Israel as the nations if they sinned like them.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witness agrees: the threat — "I shall do unto you, as I thought to do unto them," the threat (Gill); "I shall do unto you," the judgment (Gill); "as I thought to do unto them," the likeness (Amos 3:2, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the threat. The verse marks Israel: warned. The verse displays the judgment: as the nations. The verse displays the sin: punished. The verse displays the justice: impartial. The threat displays God's impartial justice on his own people. The verse displays Israel: not exempt. The verse displays the judgment: the same. The verse displays the warning: solemn. The warning that God would judge Israel as the nations if they sinned like them displays the impartial justice of God: I shall do unto you, as I thought to do unto them (Amos 3:2) — God's solemn warning that the privilege of his people is no shield to those who do the abominations of the heathen, that Israel not presume on their election but obey. The verse closes the chapter, before the borders of the promised land (ch.34).
